title: Metric Taxonomy for Valuation Driver Analysis

metrics.name: Distributable Earnings per Share
metrics.formula: Distributable earnings / diluted share count
metrics.definition_notes: Distributable earnings is a non-GAAP measure. Each firm defines DE differently — some include realized performance fees, others exclude them. Some firms label this as 'After-tax Distributable Earnings' or 'Realized Income.' Always record the firm's specific DE definition alongside the value.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings release, 10-K/20-F, earnings supplement
metrics.known_issues: Significant definition heterogeneity across firms. Some firms deduct equity-based compensation from DE, others do not. Tax treatment varies (pre-tax vs. after-tax DE). Must normalize or document firm-by-firm definitions.

metrics.name: Earnings per Share (GAAP)
metrics.formula: GAAP net income attributable to common shareholders / diluted share count
metrics.definition_notes: Standard GAAP EPS as reported. For firms reporting under IFRS (e.g., non-US listed managers), use IFRS basic/diluted EPS. GAAP EPS for alt managers is highly volatile due to mark-to-market on carried interest and investment income.
metrics.primary_data_source: 10-K/20-F income statement
metrics.known_issues: GAAP EPS for alternative asset managers is notoriously volatile and often negative during drawdowns due to unrealized investment losses. This makes it a noisy signal — included for completeness but expected to be a weaker driver than DE/share.

metrics.name: DE/Share Growth, 3-Year CAGR
metrics.formula: (DE_share_t / DE_share_t-3)^(1/3) - 1
metrics.definition_notes: Compound annual growth rate of distributable earnings per share over a trailing 3-year period. Requires at least 4 data points (current year + 3 prior years). If DE was negative in the base year, this metric is undefined — record as N/A.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from historical DE/share data across multiple annual filings
metrics.known_issues: Undefined when base-year DE is negative or zero. Firms that recently IPO'd may not have 3 years of comparable DE data. M&A activity can distort growth rates if share count changed materially.

metrics.name: Fee-Earning AUM
metrics.formula: As disclosed by each firm; typically the subset of total AUM on which the firm earns management fees
metrics.definition_notes: FEAUM represents AUM that generates management fee revenue. Definitions vary: some firms include committed but uninvested capital (on committed capital basis), others only count invested capital (on invested capital basis). Some firms use 'Fee-Paying AUM' or 'Fee-Generating AUM' as equivalent terms.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings supplement, investor presentation, quarterly earnings release
metrics.known_issues: Definition heterogeneity: committed-capital vs. invested-capital basis. Some firms include advisory/sub-advisory AUM that earns lower fees. Currency effects for non-USD denominated funds. Must convert to common currency (USD) at period-end exchange rates.

metrics.name: Total AUM
metrics.formula: Total assets under management as disclosed by the firm, including both fee-earning and non-fee-earning assets
metrics.definition_notes: Broadest measure of scale. Includes AUM that does not yet generate fees (e.g., dry powder, co-investment capital that may not charge fees). Some firms include 'Assets Under Advisement' in total AUM — exclude these if separately disclosed.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings supplement, investor presentation
metrics.known_issues: Less standardized than FEAUM. Double-counting risk if fund-of-funds or multi-strategy vehicles are included. Some firms inflate AUM by including leverage at the fund level.

metrics.name: FEAUM Growth, Year-over-Year
metrics.formula: (FEAUM_t / FEAUM_t-1) - 1
metrics.definition_notes: Year-over-year percentage change in fee-earning AUM. Captures both organic growth and M&A-driven AUM additions. Use end-of-fiscal-year values for both periods.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from FEAUM values across two consecutive annual periods
metrics.known_issues: Includes both organic and inorganic (M&A) growth, making it less clean as a signal. Large acquisitions can cause one-time jumps. FX movements affect USD-denominated FEAUM for firms with non-USD funds.

metrics.name: FEAUM Growth, 3-Year CAGR
metrics.formula: (FEAUM_t / FEAUM_t-3)^(1/3) - 1
metrics.definition_notes: Compound annual growth rate of FEAUM over a trailing 3-year period. Smooths out year-to-year volatility and provides a medium-term growth trajectory. Requires 4 data points.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from historical FEAUM data across multiple annual filings
metrics.known_issues: Same issues as FEAUM YoY (includes inorganic growth). Firms that recently IPO'd or underwent major restructuring may have non-comparable base-year figures.

metrics.name: Net Organic FEAUM Growth
metrics.formula: (Gross inflows - outflows - realizations) / beginning-of-period FEAUM. Excludes M&A-driven AUM additions and market appreciation/depreciation.
metrics.definition_notes: Isolates management's ability to attract and retain capital organically. The cleanest measure of franchise strength. Some firms disclose this directly; for others, it must be estimated by subtracting disclosed acquisition-related AUM additions and market effects from total FEAUM change.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings supplement, quarterly earnings call, AUM bridge/waterfall disclosures
metrics.known_issues: Not all firms provide a clean organic growth figure. Estimating it requires decomposing the AUM bridge into organic inflows, market effects, realizations, and M&A. Realization-heavy periods (fund harvesting) can depress organic growth even when fundraising is strong.

metrics.name: Fundraising as Percentage of AUM
metrics.formula: Total capital raised in period / beginning-of-period AUM * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Measures fundraising intensity relative to existing scale. Higher values indicate strong LP demand. 'Total capital raised' includes new fund closes, continuation vehicles, and incremental commitments to existing open-end vehicles, but excludes reinvested distributions.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings release, investor day presentations, quarterly supplements
metrics.known_issues: Fundraising is lumpy — large flagship fund closes create spikes. Annual figure may not reflect steady-state fundraising capacity. Some firms do not separately disclose total capital raised, requiring estimation from AUM bridges.

metrics.name: Asset Class Herfindahl-Hirschman Index
metrics.formula: Sum(share_i^2) * 10,000, where share_i is the fraction of total AUM in asset class i. Asset classes: private equity, credit, real assets/infrastructure, hedge fund solutions, insurance solutions, other.
metrics.definition_notes: Measures concentration of AUM across asset classes. HHI = 10,000 means 100% in one asset class (maximum concentration). HHI < 2,500 indicates moderate diversification. Lower HHI may signal more stable, diversified fee streams. Use each firm's own asset class breakdown as the basis, then map to a standardized set of 5-7 categories for comparability.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from AUM breakdown by asset class, typically found in investor presentations and earnings supplements
metrics.known_issues: Asset class definitions vary across firms. Mapping to a standardized taxonomy introduces subjectivity. Some firms bundle strategies differently (e.g., 'real assets' vs. separate 'real estate' and 'infrastructure').

metrics.name: Percentage of Permanent Capital
metrics.formula: Perpetual/open-end/evergreen AUM / total AUM * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Permanent capital includes: publicly listed vehicles (BDCs, REITs), perpetual/open-end private funds, insurance company general accounts, and long-duration vehicles with no fixed end date. Higher permanent capital percentage implies more predictable, durable management fee streams with lower re-fundraising risk.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings supplement, investor day presentations, annual report commentary
metrics.known_issues: Definition of 'permanent' varies — some firms include vehicles with 15+ year terms as 'perpetual.' Others only count truly open-ended structures. Must apply a consistent definition across the sample. Some firms report 'long-duration' capital which may overlap but is not identical.

metrics.name: Percentage of AUM in Credit Strategies
metrics.formula: Credit AUM / total AUM * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Credit strategies include: direct lending, distressed debt, structured credit (CLOs), mezzanine, special situations (credit-oriented), and investment-grade private credit. Excludes equity-oriented special situations. Credit has emerged as the fastest-growing segment in alternatives, so high credit exposure may signal growth optionality.
metrics.primary_data_source: AUM breakdown disclosure in earnings supplements and investor presentations
metrics.known_issues: Boundary between 'credit' and 'special situations' or 'opportunistic' strategies is blurry. Some firms include insurance-related fixed income in credit AUM. Must apply consistent classification.

metrics.name: Fee-Related Earnings Margin
metrics.formula: FRE / management fee revenue * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Measures operating leverage on the fee-earning business, excluding performance/incentive income. FRE is a non-GAAP measure. Some firms define FRE as management fees minus operating expenses (excluding performance-related compensation). Others include transaction/advisory fees in the FRE numerator. Always document the specific FRE definition used by each firm.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings release, 10-K/20-F segment disclosure, earnings supplement
metrics.known_issues: Significant definition heterogeneity. Key differences: (1) whether equity-based compensation is deducted, (2) whether transaction/advisory fees are included in revenue, (3) treatment of distribution/placement costs. Must document each firm's FRE definition. Margins are not directly comparable without normalization notes.

metrics.name: FRE Growth, Year-over-Year
metrics.formula: (FRE_t / FRE_t-1) - 1
metrics.definition_notes: Year-over-year percentage change in Fee-Related Earnings. Captures both revenue growth and margin expansion/contraction. Use the same FRE definition consistently across periods for each firm.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from FRE across two consecutive annual periods
metrics.known_issues: If FRE was negative in the prior year, growth rate is undefined. M&A-driven revenue additions inflate FRE growth. One-time items (e.g., catch-up fees on new fund activations) can distort.

metrics.name: Compensation-to-Revenue Ratio
metrics.formula: Total compensation and benefits expense / total revenue * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Measures what share of revenue is consumed by employee compensation. Lower ratios indicate greater operating leverage. 'Total compensation' includes base salary, cash bonuses, equity-based compensation, and benefits. 'Total revenue' includes management fees, performance/incentive fees, and other operating revenue.
metrics.primary_data_source: 10-K/20-F income statement, segment disclosures
metrics.known_issues: Revenue denominator is volatile due to performance fees. A better approach may be to compute comp ratio on fee revenue only. Equity-based compensation treatment varies (some firms expense it through P&L, others adjust it out for non-GAAP measures). Partnership structures may obscure true compensation costs.

metrics.name: Management Fee Rate
metrics.formula: Management fee revenue / average FEAUM * 10,000
metrics.definition_notes: Blended average management fee rate across all fee-earning strategies. Use average FEAUM (beginning + end of period / 2) to avoid timing distortions. Higher fee rates indicate pricing power and/or a mix tilted toward higher-fee strategies (e.g., PE vs. credit). Declining fee rates may signal competitive pressure or mix shift toward lower-fee products.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from management fee revenue (earnings release) and FEAUM
metrics.known_issues: Blended rate masks strategy-level fee variation. A firm growing fast in lower-fee credit may show declining blended rate even if strategy-level rates are stable. Catch-up fees or retroactive fee adjustments in a quarter can distort annualized rates.

metrics.name: Performance Fee Share of Total Revenue
metrics.formula: (Performance/incentive fees + realized carried interest allocations) / total revenue * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Measures revenue cyclicality/upside optionality. Higher performance fee share means more volatile but potentially higher-upside revenue. Includes both realized carried interest and incentive fees from hedge fund/credit vehicles. Excludes unrealized carried interest (which affects GAAP but not cash economics).
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings release, income statement, segment disclosure
metrics.known_issues: Highly volatile year-to-year depending on fund vintage harvesting cycles. A single large realization can spike this metric. Market may discount high performance fee share (preferring stable management fees) or reward it (as optionality). Direction of correlation with valuation is theoretically ambiguous.

metrics.name: Management Fee Revenue
metrics.formula: Total management fee revenue as reported for the fiscal year
metrics.definition_notes: Absolute dollar amount of management fees collected. The most stable and recurring revenue line for alternative asset managers. Includes base management fees, transaction/monitoring fees if classified under management fees, and may include fund administration fees depending on the firm.
metrics.primary_data_source: Earnings release, 10-K/20-F revenue breakdown
metrics.known_issues: Some firms bundle advisory/transaction fees with management fees; others report them separately. Must ensure consistent treatment across firms. Currency effects for non-USD reporting firms.

metrics.name: Management Fee Revenue Growth, Year-over-Year
metrics.formula: (Management fee revenue_t / Management fee revenue_t-1) - 1
metrics.definition_notes: Year-over-year growth in management fee revenue. The most direct measure of recurring revenue momentum. Driven by both FEAUM growth and fee rate changes.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed from management fee revenue across two consecutive annual periods
metrics.known_issues: M&A can inflate growth rates. Currency effects for globally diversified firms. Fee holiday periods on new funds can depress revenue relative to FEAUM growth.

metrics.name: Price-to-Fee-Related Earnings
metrics.formula: Market capitalization / annual Fee-Related Earnings
metrics.definition_notes: The most widely used valuation multiple for publicly listed alternative asset managers. Prices the recurring, fee-based earnings stream. Market cap = share price * diluted shares outstanding (use treasury stock method). FRE should be the full-year figure on a consistent basis with the firm's reporting.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed: market cap from stock price (as of fiscal year-end or a recent date) * diluted shares; FRE from earnings
metrics.known_issues: FRE definition heterogeneity (see MET-VD-013). Negative FRE makes this metric undefined. Share price date must be consistent across the sample (use same reference date for all firms). For firms with multiple share classes, use total economic market cap.

metrics.name: Price-to-Distributable Earnings
metrics.formula: Market capitalization / annual Distributable Earnings
metrics.definition_notes: Prices the total cash earnings stream including both fee-related and realized performance income. Broader than P/FRE — captures the full economic earnings power. Market cap = share price * diluted shares outstanding.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed: market cap from stock price * diluted shares; DE from earnings release
metrics.known_issues: DE definition heterogeneity (see MET-VD-001). DE includes realized performance fees which are volatile, making P/DE less stable than P/FRE across periods. Negative DE makes this undefined. Same share price dating requirement as P/FRE.

metrics.name: Enterprise Value-to-Fee-Earning AUM
metrics.formula: (Market capitalization + total debt - cash and equivalents) / FEAUM * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Measures how much the market pays per dollar of fee-earning AUM. Expressed in percentage terms (e.g., 5% means $0.05 of EV per $1 of FEAUM). Enterprise value accounts for capital structure differences across firms. Use net debt = total debt - cash and short-term investments. Exclude fund-level debt (non-recourse); include only corporate-level debt.
metrics.primary_data_source: Computed: market cap from stock price * shares; debt and cash from balance sheet; FEAUM from earnings supplement
metrics.known_issues: Debt classification can be tricky — must separate corporate debt from fund-level non-recourse debt. Some firms carry significant balance sheet co-investments which complicate EV calculation. Minority interests in fund management subsidiaries may need adjustment.

metrics.name: Average Daily Trading Volume
metrics.formula: Average number of shares traded per day over the trailing 90-day period
metrics.definition_notes: Contextual liquidity measure. Higher ADTV may be associated with tighter bid-ask spreads and greater institutional ownership, but it is a consequence of market interest rather than an actionable management lever.
metrics.primary_data_source: Bloomberg, Yahoo Finance, exchange data
metrics.known_issues: Not a driver — excluded from correlation analysis. Included for descriptive context only. Rationale for exclusion: ADTV is a consequence of investor demand and index inclusion, not a fundamental characteristic that management can influence to drive valuation.

metrics.name: Passive Ownership Percentage
metrics.formula: Shares held by passive/index funds / total shares outstanding * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Contextual ownership structure measure. High passive ownership may indicate index inclusion and can affect price dynamics, but is not an actionable management lever.
metrics.primary_data_source: 13F filings, Bloomberg ownership data, Morningstar
metrics.known_issues: Not a driver — excluded from correlation analysis. Rationale for exclusion: passive ownership is a consequence of index inclusion decisions and market cap thresholds, not a fundamental characteristic. Identifying which holders are truly 'passive' requires judgment.

metrics.name: Free Float Percentage
metrics.formula: (Total shares outstanding - insider/founder/strategic holdings) / total shares outstanding * 100
metrics.definition_notes: Contextual measure of trading availability. Many alt managers have significant insider/founder ownership which reduces free float. Low free float can affect valuation multiples through liquidity discount but is a structural artifact, not a management-actionable driver.
metrics.primary_data_source: Proxy statement, Bloomberg, exchange filings
metrics.known_issues: Not a driver — excluded from correlation analysis. Rationale for exclusion: free float is a structural artifact of founding/ownership history. Defining 'insider' holdings requires judgment — include board members, executives, and known strategic/family holders.

correlation_design.rationale: Non-parametric, robust to outliers and non-normality, appropriate for small N (~25). Does not require distributional assumptions that make regression unreliable at this sample size.
correlation_design.significance_note: With N~25, Spearman rho of ~0.40 is statistically significant at p<0.05 (two-tailed). Report both rho and p-value for each test. Consider Bonferroni or FDR correction given multiple comparisons (57 tests).
correlation_design.stable_driver_criterion: rho > 0.5 across all three multiples with p < 0.05
correlation_design.multiple_specific_criterion: rho > 0.5 for exactly one multiple with p < 0.05
correlation_design.moderate_signal_criterion: 0.3 <= rho <= 0.5 for at least one multiple
correlation_design.multiple_testing_correction: Benjamini-Hochberg FDR at q=0.10 to balance power and false discovery control given exploratory nature of the analysis
